The **House of Lords**[[a]](#cite_note-9) is the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom").[[6]](#cite_note-10) Like the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), it meets in the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England.[[7]](#cite_note-11) One of the oldest extant [institutions](/wiki/Institution "Institution") in the world, its origins lie in the early 11th century and the emergence of [bicameralism](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") in the 14th century.[[8]](#cite_note-12)[[9]](#cite_note-13)[[10]](#cite_note-14)[[11]](#cite_note-15)[[12]](#cite_note-16)

In contrast to the House of Commons, membership of the Lords is not generally acquired by [election](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "Elections in the United Kingdom"). Most members are [appointed for life](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer"), on either a political or non-political basis.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-17)[[14]](#cite_note-Institute_for_Government-2022-18) [Hereditary membership was limited](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999") in 1999 to 92 [excepted hereditary peers](/wiki/List_of_excepted_hereditary_peers "List of excepted hereditary peers"): 90 elected through [internal by-elections](/wiki/By-elections_to_the_House_of_Lords "By-elections to the House of Lords"), plus the [Earl Marshal](/wiki/Earl_Marshal "Earl Marshal") and the [Lord Great Chamberlain](/wiki/Lord_Great_Chamberlain "Lord Great Chamberlain") as members [*ex officio*](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member"), the sole two officers to directly inherit their seats. The hereditary peers are due to lose their seats when section 1 of the [House of Lords (Hereditary Peers) Act 2026](/wiki/House_of_Lords_(Hereditary_Peers)_Act_2026 "House of Lords (Hereditary Peers) Act 2026") comes into force. The House of Lords also includes up to 26 [archbishops and bishops of the Church of England](/wiki/List_of_bishops_in_the_Church_of_England "List of bishops in the Church of England"), known as [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual").[[14]](#cite_note-Institute_for_Government-2022-18)[[15]](#cite_note-:1-19) Since 2014, membership may be [voluntarily relinquished or terminated upon expulsion](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Reform_Act_2014 "House of Lords Reform Act 2014").[[14]](#cite_note-Institute_for_Government-2022-18)

As the upper house of Parliament, the House of Lords has many similar functions to the House of Commons.[[16]](#cite_note-20) It scrutinises [legislation](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation"), holds [the government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") to account, and considers and reports upon [public policy](/wiki/Public_policy "Public policy").[[17]](#cite_note-Parliament_UK-21) [Peers](/wiki/Peerages_in_the_United_Kingdom "Peerages in the United Kingdom") may also seek to introduce legislation or propose [amendments](/wiki/Amendment "Amendment") to [bills](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)").[[17]](#cite_note-Parliament_UK-21) While it is [unable to prevent bills passing into law](/wiki/Parliament_Acts_1911_and_1949 "Parliament Acts 1911 and 1949"), except in certain limited circumstances, it may delay the [enactment](/wiki/Enactment_(British_legal_term) "Enactment (British legal term)") of bills for up to one year.[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[19]](#cite_note-23) In this capacity, as a body independent from the pressures of the political process, the House of Lords is said to act as a "revising chamber" focusing on legislative detail, while occasionally asking the House of Commons to reconsider its plans.[[20]](#cite_note-24)[[21]](#cite_note-25)

While peers may also serve as [government ministers](/wiki/Government_ministers "Government ministers"), they are typically only selected to serve as junior ministers, except for the [Leader of the House of Lords](/wiki/Leader_of_the_House_of_Lords "Leader of the House of Lords").[[22]](#cite_note-26) The House of Lords does not control the term of the prime minister or of the government;[[23]](#cite_note-Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom-2010-27) only the Commons may vote to require the prime minister to resign or call an election.[[24]](#cite_note-28) Unlike the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), which has a defined number of seats, the number of members in the House of Lords is not fixed. As of 26 March 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit), it has [840 sitting members](/wiki/List_of_current_members_of_the_House_of_Lords "List of current members of the House of Lords").

The [King's Speech](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament#Delivery_of_the_speech_from_the_throne "State Opening of Parliament") is delivered in the House of Lords chamber during the [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament"). In addition to its role as the upper house, the House of Lords, through the [Law Lords](/wiki/Law_Lords "Law Lords"), acted as the final court of appeal in the United Kingdom judicial system until the establishment of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") in 2009.[[25]](#cite_note-29)

The House of Lords is the only upper house of any [bicameral parliament](/wiki/Bicameral_parliament "Bicameral parliament") in the world to be larger than its lower house.[[26]](#cite_note-30) It is the [second-largest legislative chamber in the world](/wiki/List_of_legislatures_by_number_of_members "List of legislatures by number of members"), behind the [National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") of China.[[27]](#cite_note-31)

The House of Lords also has a [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") role, in that [Church Measures](/wiki/Church_of_England_Assembly_(Powers)_Act_1919 "Church of England Assembly (Powers) Act 1919") must be tabled within the House by the Lords Spiritual. The United Kingdom is one of only three countries in the world to award religious figures a permanent seat in the legislature, the others being Iran and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Lords_chamber_from_above_%28SO%2724%29.jpg/500px-Lords_chamber_from_above_%28SO%2724%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lords_chamber_from_above_(SO%2724).jpg)

The constitutional concept of the [King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament") is embodied within the chamber at the [State Opening](/wiki/State_Opening "State Opening") which inaugurates each [parliamentary session](/wiki/Parliamentary_session "Parliamentary session") (as in 2024, above).

Today's Parliament of the United Kingdom largely descends, in practice, from the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), through the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") of 1706 and the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union,_1707 "Acts of Union, 1707") that implemented and executed the Treaty in 1707 and created a new [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") to replace the Parliament of England and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"). This new parliament was, in effect, the continuation of the Parliament of England with the addition of 45 [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MPs) and 16 Peers to represent Scotland.

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The House of Lords developed from the "Great Council" (*[Magnum Concilium](/wiki/Magnum_Concilium "Magnum Concilium")*) that advised the king during medieval times, dating back to the early 11th century.[[28]](#cite_note-loveland1-32) This royal council came to be composed of ecclesiastics, noblemen, and representatives of the [counties of England](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") and [Wales](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_Wales "Historic counties of Wales") (afterwards, representatives of the [boroughs](/wiki/Parliamentary_borough "Parliamentary borough") as well). The first English Parliament is often considered to be either [Simon de Montfort's Parliament](/wiki/Simon_de_Montfort%27s_Parliament "Simon de Montfort's Parliament") (held in 1265) or the "[Model Parliament](/wiki/Model_Parliament "Model Parliament")" (held in 1295), which included archbishops, bishops, abbots, earls, barons, and representatives of the shires and boroughs.

The power of Parliament grew slowly, fluctuating as the strength of the monarchy grew or declined. For example, during much of the reign of [Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II") (1307–1327), the [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") was supreme, the [Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") weak, and the shire and borough representatives entirely powerless.

During the reign of King Edward II's successor, [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III") (1327–1377), Parliament clearly separated into two distinct [chambers](/wiki/Debating_chamber "Debating chamber"): the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England") (consisting of the shire and borough representatives) and the House of Lords (consisting of the archbishops, bishops, abbots and nobility).[[29]](#cite_note-33) The authority of Parliament continued to grow, and during the early 15th century both Houses exercised powers to an extent not seen before. The Lords were far more powerful than the Commons because of the great influence of the great landowners and the prelates of the realm.

The power of the nobility declined during the civil wars of the late 15th century, known as the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses"). Much of the nobility was killed on the battlefield or executed for participation in the war, and many aristocratic estates were lost to the Crown. Moreover, [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") was dying, and the [feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") armies controlled by the [barons](/wiki/Baron "Baron") became obsolete. [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") (1485–1509) clearly established the supremacy of the monarch, symbolised by the "Crown Imperial". The domination of the Sovereign continued to grow during the reigns of the [Tudor monarchs](/wiki/Tudor_dynasty "Tudor dynasty") in the 16th century. The Crown was at the height of its power during the reign of [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") (1509–1547).

### 17th–18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 17th–18th century")]

The House of Lords remained more powerful than the House of Commons, but the lower house continued to grow in influence, reaching a zenith in relation to the House of Lords during the middle of the 17th century. Conflicts between the king and the Parliament (for the most part, the House of Commons) ultimately led to the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") during the 1640s. In 1649, after the defeat and execution of [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") was declared, but the nation was effectively under the overall control of [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), [Lord Protector of England, Scotland and Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector").

The House of Lords was reduced to a largely powerless body, with Cromwell and his supporters in the Commons dominating the government. On 19 March 1649, the House of Lords was abolished by an act of Parliament, which declared that "The Commons of England [find] by too long experience that the House of Lords is useless and dangerous to the people of England."[[30]](#cite_note-34) It was briefly de facto eclipsed by [Cromwell's Other House](/wiki/Cromwell%27s_Other_House "Cromwell's Other House"). The House of Lords did not assemble again until the [Convention Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1660) "Convention Parliament (1660)") met in 1660 and the monarchy was restored. It returned to its former position as the more powerful chamber of Parliament—a position it would occupy until the 19th century.

After the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), the [peerage of Scotland](/wiki/Peerage_of_Scotland "Peerage of Scotland") elected sixteen of their number, the [Scottish representative peers](/wiki/Scottish_representative_peers "Scottish representative peers"), to sit in the House of Lords. General elections were held with each Parliament, and by-elections to fill vacancies in between. The elections ceased after the [Peerage Act 1963](/wiki/Peerage_Act_1963 "Peerage Act 1963") granted all peers of Scotland a hereditary seat in the House of Lords.

The first [election of Scottish representative peers](/wiki/List_of_elections_of_Scottish_representative_peers "List of elections of Scottish representative peers") took place on 15 February 1707 at the [Parliament House, Edinburgh](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Edinburgh "Parliament House, Edinburgh"), shortly before the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") was adjourned for the last time on 25 March. The [commissioners](/wiki/Commissioner_(Scottish_Parliament) "Commissioner (Scottish Parliament)") for the barons and the burghs chose [their representatives](/wiki/Scottish_representatives_to_the_first_Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Scottish representatives to the first Parliament of Great Britain") to the British House of Commons at the same time.[[31]](#cite_note-35)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

The 19th century was marked by several changes to the House of Lords. The House, once a body of only about 50 members, had been greatly enlarged by the liberality of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") and his successors in creating peerages. The individual influence of a Lord of Parliament was thus diminished.

Moreover, the power of the House as a whole decreased, whilst that of the House of Commons grew. Particularly notable in the development of the Lower House's superiority was the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"). The electoral system of the House of Commons was far from democratic: property qualifications greatly restricted the size of the electorate, and the boundaries of many constituencies had not been changed for centuries. Entire cities such as [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") had not even one representative in the House of Commons, while the 11 voters of [Old Sarum](/wiki/Old_Sarum_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Old Sarum (UK Parliament constituency)") retained their ancient right to elect two MPs despite living elsewhere. A small borough was susceptible to bribery, and was often under the control of a patron, whose nominee was guaranteed to win an election. Some aristocrats were patrons of numerous "[pocket boroughs](/wiki/Pocket_boroughs "Pocket boroughs")", and therefore controlled a considerable part of the membership of the House of Commons.

When the House of Commons passed a Reform Bill to correct some of these anomalies in 1831, the House of Lords rejected the proposal. The popular cause of reform, however, was not abandoned by the ministry, despite a second rejection of the bill in 1832. Prime Minister [Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey") advised the King to overwhelm opposition to the bill in the House of Lords by creating about 80 new pro-Reform peers. [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") originally balked at the proposal, which effectively threatened the opposition of the House of Lords, but at length relented.

Before the new peers were created, however, the Lords who opposed the bill admitted defeat and abstained from the vote, allowing the passage of the bill. The crisis damaged the political influence of the House of Lords but did not altogether end it. A vital reform was effected by the Lords themselves in 1868, when they changed their standing orders to abolish proxy voting, preventing Lords from voting without taking the trouble to attend.[[32]](#cite_note-36) Over the course of the century the powers of the upper house were further reduced stepwise, culminating in the 20th century with the [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911"); the Commons gradually became the stronger House of Parliament.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

The status of the House of Lords returned to the forefront of debate after the election of a [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") Government in 1906. In 1909 the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), introduced into the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") the "[People's Budget](/wiki/People%27s_Budget "People's Budget")", which proposed a land tax targeting wealthy landowners. The popular measure, however, was defeated in the heavily Conservative House of Lords.[[33]](#cite_note-37)

Having made the powers of the House of Lords a primary campaign issue, the Liberals were narrowly re-elected in [January 1910](/wiki/January_1910_United_Kingdom_general_election "January 1910 United Kingdom general election"). The Liberals had lost most of their support in the Lords, which was routinely rejecting Liberals' bills. Prime Minister [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith") then proposed that the powers of the House of Lords be severely curtailed. After a further [general election in December 1910](/wiki/December_1910_United_Kingdom_general_election "December 1910 United Kingdom general election"), and with a reluctant promise by King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") to create sufficient new Liberal peers to overcome the Lords' opposition to the measure if necessary, the Asquith Government secured the passage of a bill to curtail the powers of the House of Lords.[[34]](#cite_note-38) The [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911") effectively abolished the power of the House of Lords to reject legislation, or to amend it in a way unacceptable to the House of Commons; and most bills could be delayed for no more than three parliamentary sessions or two calendar years. It was not meant to be a permanent solution; more comprehensive reforms were planned.[[35]](#cite_note-39)[[36]](#cite_note-40) Neither party, however, pursued reforms with much enthusiasm, and the House of Lords remained primarily hereditary. The [Parliament Act 1949](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1949 "Parliament Act 1949") reduced the delaying power of the House of Lords further to two sessions or one year. In 1958, the predominantly hereditary nature of the House of Lords was changed by the [Life Peerages Act 1958](/wiki/Life_Peerages_Act_1958 "Life Peerages Act 1958"), which authorised the creation of life baronies, with no numerical limits. The number of life peers then gradually increased, though not at a constant rate.[[37]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/41/1911-New-Perrs-UK-Punch.jpg)](/wiki/File:1911-New-Perrs-UK-Punch.jpg)

*Punch* 1911 cartoon shows Asquith and Lloyd George preparing coronets for 500 new peers to threaten takeover of the House of Lords.

The Labour Party had, for most of the 20th century, a commitment, based on the party's historic opposition to class privilege, to abolish the House of Lords, or at least expel the hereditary element. In 1968 the Labour Government of [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") attempted to reform the House of Lords by introducing a system under which hereditary peers would be allowed to remain in the House and take part in debate, but would be unable to vote. This plan, however, was defeated in the House of Commons by a coalition of traditionalist Conservatives (such as [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell")), and Labour members who continued to advocate the outright abolition of the Upper House (such as [Michael Foot](/wiki/Michael_Foot "Michael Foot")).

When Foot became leader of the Labour Party in 1980, abolition of the House of Lords became a part of the party's agenda; under his successor, [Neil Kinnock](/wiki/Neil_Kinnock "Neil Kinnock"), however, a reformed Upper House was proposed instead. In the meantime, the creation of new hereditary peerages (except for members of the Royal Family) has been arrested, with the exception of three that were created during the administration of Conservative PM [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") in the 1980s.

Whilst some hereditary peers were at best apathetic, the Labour Party's clear commitments were not lost on [Merlin Hanbury-Tracy, 7th Baron Sudeley](/wiki/Merlin_Hanbury-Tracy,_7th_Baron_Sudeley "Merlin Hanbury-Tracy, 7th Baron Sudeley"), who for decades was considered an expert on the House of Lords. In December 1979 the [Conservative Monday Club](/wiki/Conservative_Monday_Club "Conservative Monday Club") published his extensive paper entitled *Lords Reform – Why tamper with the House of Lords?* and in July 1980 [*The Monarchist*](/wiki/International_Monarchist_League "International Monarchist League") carried another article by Sudeley entitled "Why Reform or Abolish the House of Lords?".[[38]](#cite_note-42) In 1990 he wrote a further booklet for the Monday Club entitled "The Preservation of the House of Lords".

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2019, a seven-month enquiry by Naomi Ellenbogen found that one in five staff of the House had experienced bullying or harassment which they did not report for fear of reprisals.[[39]](#cite_note-43) This was preceded by several cases, including Liberal Democrat [Anthony Lester, Lord Lester of Herne Hill](/wiki/Anthony_Lester,_Baron_Lester_of_Herne_Hill "Anthony Lester, Baron Lester of Herne Hill"), of Lords using their position to sexually harass or abuse women.[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45)

In 2020, the [Johnson government](/wiki/Second_Johnson_ministry "Second Johnson ministry") considered moving the House of Lords from London to a city in [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), likely [York](/wiki/York "York"), or [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), in the [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands "Midlands"), in an attempt to "reconnect" the area. It was unclear how the [King's Speech](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") would be conducted in the event of a move.[[42]](#cite_note-46)[[43]](#cite_note-47)[[44]](#cite_note-48) The idea was received negatively by many peers.[[45]](#cite_note-49)

## Reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reforms")]

Main article: [Reform of the House of Lords](/wiki/Reform_of_the_House_of_Lords "Reform of the House of Lords")

With the advent of democratic politics in the United Kingdom, beginning with the [Reform Acts](/wiki/Reform_Acts "Reform Acts") from 1832 to 1928, the aristocratic House of Lords was increasingly perceived as an [anachronism](/wiki/Anachronism "Anachronism"). Many attempts to reform it have been made, and some have succeeded, most notably the removal of most [hereditary peers](/wiki/Hereditary_peer "Hereditary peer") in 1999.

### First admission of women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: First admission of women")]

Main article: [Women in the House of Lords](/wiki/Women_in_the_House_of_Lords "Women in the House of Lords")

There were no women sitting in the House of Lords until 1958, when a small number came into the chamber as a result of the [Life Peerages Act 1958](/wiki/Life_Peerages_Act_1958 "Life Peerages Act 1958"). One of these was [Irene Curzon, 2nd Baroness Ravensdale](/wiki/Irene_Curzon,_2nd_Baroness_Ravensdale "Irene Curzon, 2nd Baroness Ravensdale"), who had inherited her father's peerage in 1925 and was made a life peer to enable her to sit. After a campaign stretching back in some cases to the 1920s, another twelve women who held hereditary peerages in their own right were admitted with the passage of the [Peerage Act 1963](/wiki/Peerage_Act_1963 "Peerage Act 1963").

### New Labour era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: New Labour era")]

The Labour Party included in its 1997 general election [manifesto](/wiki/Manifesto "Manifesto") a commitment to remove the hereditary peerage from the House of Lords.[[46]](#cite_note-50) Their subsequent election victory in 1997 under [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") led to the denouement of the traditional House of Lords. The Labour government introduced legislation to expel all hereditary peers from the Upper House as a first step in Lords reform. As a part of a compromise, however, it agreed to permit 92 hereditary peers to remain until the reforms were complete. Thus, all but 92 hereditary peers were expelled under the [House of Lords Act 1999](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999") (see below for its provisions), making the House of Lords predominantly an appointed house.[[47]](#cite_note-51)

In 2000, the [Wakeham Commission](/wiki/Wakeham_Commission "Wakeham Commission") proposed introducing a 20% elected element to the Lords, but this plan was widely criticised.[[48]](#cite_note-52) A [parliamentary Joint Committee](/wiki/Parliamentary_Joint_Committee "Parliamentary Joint Committee") was established in 2001 to resolve the issue, but it reached no conclusion and instead gave Parliament seven options to choose from (fully appointed, 20% elected, 40% elected, 50% elected, 60% elected, 80% elected, and fully elected). In a confusing series of votes in February 2003, all of these options were defeated, although the 80% elected option fell by just three votes in the Commons.[[49]](#cite_note-53) Socialist MPs favouring outright abolition voted against all the options.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

In 2005, a cross-party group of senior MPs ([Kenneth Clarke](/wiki/Kenneth_Clarke "Kenneth Clarke"), [Paul Tyler](/wiki/Paul_Tyler "Paul Tyler"), [Tony Wright](/wiki/Tony_Wright_(Staffordshire_politician) "Tony Wright (Staffordshire politician)"), [George Young](/wiki/Sir_George_Young,_6th_Baronet "Sir George Young, 6th Baronet"), and [Robin Cook](/wiki/Robin_Cook "Robin Cook")) published a report proposing that 70% of members of the House of Lords should be elected – each member for a single long term – by the [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") system. Most of the remainder were to be appointed by a Commission to ensure a mix of "skills, knowledge and experience". This proposal was also not implemented. A cross-party campaign initiative called "[Elect the Lords](/wiki/Elect_the_Lords "Elect the Lords")" was set up to make the case for a predominantly elected Upper Chamber in the run up to the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election").

At the 2005 election, the Labour Party proposed further reform of the Lords, but without specific details.[[51]](#cite_note-55) The Conservative Party, which had, prior to 1997, opposed any tampering with the House of Lords,[[52]](#cite_note-56) favoured an 80% elected Lords, while the Liberal Democrats called for a fully elected [Senate](/wiki/Senate "Senate"). During 2006, a cross-party committee discussed Lords reform, with the aim of reaching a consensus: its [findings](/wiki/Reform_of_the_House_of_Lords#2007_white_paper "Reform of the House of Lords") were published in early 2007.[[53]](#cite_note-57)

On 7 March 2007, members of the House of Commons voted ten times on a variety of alternative compositions for the Upper Chamber.[[54]](#cite_note-58) Outright abolition, a wholly appointed, a 20% elected, a 40% elected, a 50% elected, and a 60% elected House of Lords were all defeated in turn. Finally, the vote for an 80% elected Lords was won by 305 votes to 267, and the vote for a wholly elected Lords was won by an even greater margin, 337 to 224. Significantly, this last vote represented an overall majority of MPs.[[55]](#cite_note-59)

Furthermore, examination of the names of MPs voting at each division shows that, of the 305 who voted for the 80% elected option, 211 went on to vote for the 100% elected option. Given that this vote took place after the vote on 80% – whose result was already known when the vote on 100% took place – this showed a clear preference for a fully elected Upper House among those who voted for the only other option that passed. But this was nevertheless only an indicative vote, and many political and legislative hurdles remained to be overcome for supporters of an elected House of Lords. Lords, soon after, rejected this proposal and voted for an entirely appointed House of Lords.[[56]](#cite_note-60)

In July 2008, [Jack Straw](/wiki/Jack_Straw "Jack Straw"), the [Secretary of State for Justice](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Justice "Secretary of State for Justice") and [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), introduced a [white paper](/wiki/White_paper "White paper") to the House of Commons proposing to replace the House of Lords with an 80–100% elected chamber, with one third being elected at each general election, to serve a term of approximately 12–15 years.[[57]](#cite_note-61) The white paper stated that, as the peerage would be totally separated from membership of the Upper House, the name "House of Lords" would no longer be appropriate. It went on to explain that there was cross-party consensus for the Chamber to be re-titled the "Senate of the United Kingdom"; however, to ensure the debate remained on the role of the Upper House rather than its title, the white paper was neutral on the title issue.

On 30 November 2009, a *Code of Conduct for Members of the House of Lords* was agreed by them. Certain amendments were agreed by them on 30 March 2010 and on 12 June 2014.[[58]](#cite_note-62)

The [scandal over expenses](/wiki/United_Kingdom_parliamentary_expenses_scandal "United Kingdom parliamentary expenses scandal") in the Commons was at its highest pitch only six months before, and the Labourite leadership under [Janet Royall, Baroness Royall of Blaisdon](/wiki/Janet_Royall,_Baroness_Royall_of_Blaisdon "Janet Royall, Baroness Royall of Blaisdon") determined that something sympathetic should be done.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Meg Russell](/wiki/Meg_Russell "Meg Russell") stated in an article, "Is the House of Lords already reformed?", three essential features of a legitimate House of Lords: The first was that it must have adequate powers over legislation to make the government think twice before making a decision. The House of Lords, she argued, had enough power to make it relevant. (In his first year, Tony Blair was defeated 38 times in the Lords—but that was before the major reform with the House of Lords Act 1999.) Second, as to the composition of the Lords, Meg Russell suggested that the composition must be distinct from the Commons, otherwise it would render the Lords useless. Third was the perceived legitimacy of the Lords. She stated, "In general legitimacy comes with election."[[59]](#cite_note-russell-63)

### Coalition reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Coalition reforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Lord_Judge_%2851111275854%29.jpg/250px-Lord_Judge_%2851111275854%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Judge_(51111275854).jpg)

The House of Lords paid tribute to [Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh"), 12 April 2021. ([COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") precautions required the maintenance of [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing") on this occasion.)

The Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition agreed, after the 2010 general election, to outline clearly a provision for a wholly or mainly elected second chamber, elected by proportional representation. These proposals sparked a debate on 29 June 2010. As an interim measure, appointment of new peers would reflect the shares of the vote secured by the political parties in the last general election.

Detailed proposals for Lords reform, including a draft House of Lords Reform Bill, were published on 17 May 2011. These included a 300-member hybrid house, of whom 80% would be elected. A further 20% would be appointed, and reserve space would be included for some Church of England archbishops and bishops. Under the proposals, members would also serve single non-renewable terms of 15 years. Former MPs would be allowed to stand for election to the Upper House, but members of the Upper House would not be immediately allowed to become MPs.

The details of the proposal were:[[60]](#cite_note-64)

* The upper chamber shall continue to be known as the House of Lords for legislative purposes.
* The reformed House of Lords should have 300 members of whom 240 are "Elected Members" and 60 appointed "Independent Members". Up to 12 Church of England archbishops and bishops may sit in the house as *ex officio* "Lords Spiritual".
* Elected Members will serve a single, non-renewable term of 15 years.
* Elections to the reformed Lords should take place at the same time as elections to the House of Commons.
* Elected Members should be elected using the [Single Transferable Vote](/wiki/Single_Transferable_Vote "Single Transferable Vote") system of proportional representation.
* Twenty Independent Members (a third) shall take their seats within the reformed house at the same time as elected members do so, and for the same 15-year term.
* Independent Members will be appointed by the King after being proposed by the Prime Minister acting on advice of an Appointments Commission.
* There will no longer be a link between the peerage system and membership of the upper house.
* The current powers of the House of Lords would not change and the House of Commons shall retain its status as the primary House of Parliament.

The proposals were considered by a Joint Committee on House of Lords Reform made up of both MPs and Peers, which issued its final report on 23 April 2012, making the following suggestions:[[61]](#cite_note-65)

* The reformed House of Lords should have 450 members.
* Party groupings, including the Crossbenchers, should choose which of their members are retained in the transition period, with the percentage of members allotted to each group based on their share of the peers with high attendance during a given period.
* Up to 12 Lords Spiritual should be retained in a reformed House of Lords.

Deputy Prime Minister [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") introduced the [House of Lords Reform Bill 2012](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Reform_Bill_2012 "House of Lords Reform Bill 2012") on 27 June 2012[[62]](#cite_note-66) which built on proposals published on 17 May 2011.[[63]](#cite_note-House_of_Lords_reform_proposals-67) However, this Bill was abandoned[[64]](#cite_note-68) by the Government on 6 August 2012, following opposition from within the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)").

#### House of Lords Reform Act 2014

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: House of Lords Reform Act 2014")]

A [private member's bill](/wiki/Private_member%27s_bill "Private member's bill") to introduce some reforms was introduced by [Dan Byles](/wiki/Dan_Byles "Dan Byles") in 2013.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The [House of Lords Reform Act 2014](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Reform_Act_2014 "House of Lords Reform Act 2014") received [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent#United_Kingdom "Royal Assent") in 2014.[[66]](#cite_note-services.parliament.uk-70) Under the new law:

* All peers can retire or resign from the chamber (prior to this only hereditary peers could disclaim their peerages).
* Peers can be disqualified for non-attendance.
* Peers can be removed for receiving prison sentences of a year or more.[[66]](#cite_note-services.parliament.uk-70)

#### House of Lords (Expulsion and Suspension) Act 2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: House of Lords (Expulsion and Suspension) Act 2015")]

The [House of Lords (Expulsion and Suspension) Act 2015](/wiki/House_of_Lords_(Expulsion_and_Suspension)_Act_2015 "House of Lords (Expulsion and Suspension) Act 2015") authorised the House to expel or suspend members.

#### Lords Spiritual (Women) Act 2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Lords Spiritual (Women) Act 2015")]

Main article: [Lords Spiritual (Women) Act 2015](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual_(Women)_Act_2015 "Lords Spiritual (Women) Act 2015")

See also: [Women in the House of Lords](/wiki/Women_in_the_House_of_Lords "Women in the House of Lords")

This Act made provision to preferentially admit female bishops of the Church of England to the Lords Spiritual over male ones in the 10 years following its commencement (2015 to 2025, later extended to 2030). This came as a consequence of the Church of England deciding in 2014 to begin to [ordain women as bishops](/wiki/Ordination_of_women_in_the_Anglican_Communion#Church_of_England "Ordination of women in the Anglican Communion"). In 2015, [Rachel Treweek](/wiki/Rachel_Treweek "Rachel Treweek"), [Bishop of Gloucester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Gloucester "Bishop of Gloucester"), became the first woman to sit as a [Lord Spiritual](/wiki/Lord_Spiritual "Lord Spiritual") in the House of Lords due to the Act.[[67]](#cite_note-71) As of 2025, eight women bishops sit as Lords Spiritual, seven of them having been accelerated due to this Act.[[68]](#cite_note-72)

### Starmer ministry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Starmer ministry")]

As of 2024, the policy of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") is to abolish the House of Lords, and to replace it with an elected second chamber,[[69]](#cite_note-73) albeit not in the first term of a Labour government.[[70]](#cite_note-74) Following the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), [a bill](/wiki/House_of_Lords_(Hereditary_Peers)_Act_2026 "House of Lords (Hereditary Peers) Act 2026") was introduced with the aim of removing all remaining hereditary peers from the House of Lords. The Bill received Royal Assent on 18 March and the provision excluding hereditary peers will come into force at the end of the present session of Parliament.[[71]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-76)[[73]](#cite_note-77)

## Size

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Size")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/The_size_of_the_United_Kingdom_House_of_Lords_since_1998.png/500px-The_size_of_the_United_Kingdom_House_of_Lords_since_1998.png)](/wiki/File:The_size_of_the_United_Kingdom_House_of_Lords_since_1998.png)

Number of members of the House of Lords from 1998 to 2021

The size of the House of Lords has varied greatly throughout its history. The English House of Lords—then comprising 168 members—was joined at Westminster by 16 Scottish peers to represent the peerage of Scotland—a total of 184 nobles—in 1707's first [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"). A further 28 Irish members to represent the peerage of Ireland were added in 1801 to the first [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). From about 220 peers in the eighteenth century,[[74]](#cite_note-78) the house saw continued expansion. From about 850 peers in 1951/52,[[75]](#cite_note-79) the numbers rose further with more [life peers](/wiki/Life_peers "Life peers") after the Life Peerages Act 1958 and the inclusion of all Scottish peers and the first female peers in the [Peerage Act 1963](/wiki/Peerage_Act_1963 "Peerage Act 1963"). It reached a record size of 1,330 in October 1999, immediately before the major Lords reform ([House of Lords Act 1999](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999")) reduced it to 669, mostly life peers, by March 2000.[[76]](#cite_note-80)

The chamber's membership again expanded in the following decades, increasing to above eight hundred active members in 2014 and prompting further reforms in the [House of Lords Reform Act 2014](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Reform_Act_2014 "House of Lords Reform Act 2014").

In April 2011, a cross-party group of former leading politicians, including many senior members of the House of Lords, called on the Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") to stop creating new peers. He had created 117 new peers between entering office in May 2010 and leaving in July 2016, a faster rate of elevation than any PM in British history; at the same time his government had tried (in vain) to reduce the House of Commons by 50, from 650 to 600 MPs.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

In August 2014, despite there being a seating capacity for only around 230[[78]](#cite_note-BBCspace-82) to 400[[79]](#cite_note-guard-83) on the benches in the Lords chamber, the House had 774 active members (plus 54 who were not entitled to attend or vote, having been suspended or granted leave of absence). This made the House of Lords the largest parliamentary chamber in any democracy.[[79]](#cite_note-guard-83) In August 2014, former [Speaker of the House of Commons](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)") [Betty Boothroyd](/wiki/Betty_Boothroyd "Betty Boothroyd") requested that "older peers should retire gracefully" to ease the overcrowding in the House of Lords. She also criticised successive prime ministers for filling the second chamber with "lobby fodder" in an attempt to help their policies become law. She made her remarks days before a new batch of peers were due to be created and several months after the passage of the House of Lords Reform Act 2014, enabling life peers to retire or resign their seats in the House, which had previously only been possible for hereditary peers and bishops.[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)

In August 2015, when 45 more peers were created in the [Dissolution Honours](/wiki/Dissolution_Honours "Dissolution Honours"), the total number of eligible members of the Lords increased to 826. In a report entitled "Does size matter?" the BBC said: "Increasingly, yes. Critics argue the House of Lords is the second largest legislature after the [Chinese National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") and dwarfs upper houses in other bicameral democracies such as the United States (100 senators), France (348 senators), Australia (76 senators), Canada (105 appointed senators) and India (250 members). The Lords is also larger than the [Supreme People's Assembly](/wiki/Supreme_People%27s_Assembly "Supreme People's Assembly") of North Korea (687 members)", and that "Peers grumble that there is not enough room to accommodate all of their colleagues in the Chamber, where there are only about 400 seats, and say they are constantly jostling for space – particularly during high-profile sittings", but added, "On the other hand, defenders of the Lords say that it does a vital job scrutinising legislation, a lot of which has come its way from the Commons in recent years".[[82]](#cite_note-BBC_News_28_August_2015-86)

In late 2016, a Lord Speaker's committee was formed to examine the issue of overcrowding, with fears membership could swell to above 1,000, and in October 2017 the committee presented its findings. In December 2017, the Lords debated and broadly approved its report, which proposed a cap on membership at 600 peers, with a fifteen-year term limit for new peers and a "two-out, one-in" limit on new appointments. By October 2018, the Lord Speaker's committee commended the reduction in peers' numbers, noting that the rate of departures had been greater than expected, with the House of Commons [Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Select Committee](/wiki/Public_Administration_and_Constitutional_Affairs_Select_Committee "Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Select Committee") approving the progress achieved without legislation.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

By April 2019, with the retirement of nearly one hundred peers since the passage of the House of Lords Reform Act 2014, the number of active peers had been reduced to a total of 782, of whom 665 were life peers.[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-parliament.uk-89) This total, however, remains greater than the membership of 669 peers in March 2000, after implementation of the House of Lords Act 1999 removed the bulk of the hereditary peers from their seats; it is well above the proposed 600-member cap, and is still larger than the House of Commons's 650 members.

## Functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Functions")]

Brief introduction of the House of Lords

### Legislative functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Legislative functions")]

Further information: [Act of Parliament (UK)](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(UK) "Act of Parliament (UK)")

All [legislation](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation"), with the exception of [money bills](/wiki/Money_bills "Money bills"), may be introduced in either the House of Lords or House of Commons.

The House of Lords debates legislation, and has the power to amend or reject bills. However, the power of the Lords to reject a bill passed by the House of Commons is severely restricted by the Parliament Acts. Under those Acts, certain types of bills may be presented for [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent") without the consent of the House of Lords (i.e. the Commons can override the Lords' veto). The House of Lords cannot delay a money bill (a bill that, in the view of the Speaker of the House of Commons, solely concerns national taxation or public funds) for more than one month.

Other public bills cannot be delayed by the House of Lords for more than two parliamentary sessions, or one calendar year. These provisions, however, only apply to public bills that originate in the House of Commons, and cannot have the effect of extending a parliamentary term beyond five years. A further restriction is a [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)") known as the [Salisbury Convention](/wiki/Salisbury_Convention "Salisbury Convention"), which means that the House of Lords does not oppose legislation promised in the Government's election manifesto.[[86]](#cite_note-90)[[87]](#cite_note-91)

By a custom that prevailed even before the Parliament Acts, the House of Lords is further restrained insofar as financial bills are concerned. The House of Lords may neither originate a bill concerning taxation or Supply (supply of treasury or exchequer funds), nor amend a bill so as to insert a taxation or Supply-related provision. (The House of Commons, however, often waives its privileges and allows the Upper House to make amendments with financial implications.) Moreover, the Upper House may not amend any Supply Bill. The House of Lords formerly maintained the absolute power to reject a bill relating to revenue or Supply, but this power was curtailed by the Parliament Acts.

### Relationship with the government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Relationship with the government")]

The House of Lords does not control the term of the prime minister or of the government.[[23]](#cite_note-Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom-2010-27) Only the lower house may force the prime minister to resign or call elections by [passing a motion of no-confidence](/wiki/Motions_of_no_confidence_in_the_United_Kingdom "Motions of no confidence in the United Kingdom") or by [withdrawing supply](/wiki/Loss_of_supply "Loss of supply"). Therefore, the House of Lords' oversight of the government is limited.

Most Cabinet ministers are from the House of Commons rather than the House of Lords. In particular, all prime ministers since 1902 have been members of the lower house[[88]](#cite_note-wasson-92) ([Alec Douglas-Home](/wiki/Alec_Douglas-Home "Alec Douglas-Home"), who became prime minister in 1963 whilst still an earl, disclaimed his peerage and was elected to the Commons soon after his term began). In recent history, it has been very rare for major cabinet positions (except Lord Chancellor and [Leader of the House of Lords](/wiki/Leader_of_the_House_of_Lords "Leader of the House of Lords")) to have been filled by peers.

Exceptions include:

* [Lord Carrington](/wiki/Lord_Carrington "Lord Carrington"), who was the [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence") from 1970 to 1974, [Secretary of State for Energy](/wiki/Department_of_Energy_(United_Kingdom) "Department of Energy (United Kingdom)") briefly for two months in early 1974 and [Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Affairs "Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs") between 1979 and 1982
* [Lord Cockfield](/wiki/Lord_Cockfield "Lord Cockfield"), who served as [Secretary of State for Trade](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Trade "Secretary of State for Trade") and [President of the Board of Trade](/wiki/President_of_the_Board_of_Trade "President of the Board of Trade")
* [Lord Young of Graffham](/wiki/Lord_Young_of_Graffham "Lord Young of Graffham"), who was [Minister without Portfolio](/wiki/Minister_without_portfolio_(United_Kingdom) "Minister without portfolio (United Kingdom)"), then [Secretary of State for Employment](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Employment "Secretary of State for Employment") and then [Secretary of State for Trade and Industry](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Trade_and_Industry "Secretary of State for Trade and Industry") and [President of the Board of Trade](/wiki/President_of_the_Board_of_Trade "President of the Board of Trade") from 1984 to 1989
* [Baroness Amos](/wiki/Baroness_Amos "Baroness Amos"), who served as [Secretary of State for International Development](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_International_Development "Secretary of State for International Development")
* [Lord Adonis](/wiki/Lord_Adonis "Lord Adonis"), who served as [Secretary of State for Transport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Transport "Secretary of State for Transport")
* [Peter Mandelson](/wiki/Peter_Mandelson "Peter Mandelson"), who served as [First Secretary of State](/wiki/First_Secretary_of_State "First Secretary of State"), [Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Business,_Innovation_and_Skills "Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills") and [President of the Board of Trade](/wiki/President_of_the_Board_of_Trade "President of the Board of Trade")
* [George Robertson](/wiki/George_Robertson,_Baron_Robertson_of_Port_Ellen "George Robertson, Baron Robertson of Port Ellen") was briefly a peer whilst serving as [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence") before resigning to take up the post of [Secretary General of NATO](/wiki/Secretary_General_of_NATO "Secretary General of NATO").
* [Baroness Morgan of Cotes](/wiki/Baroness_Morgan_of_Cotes "Baroness Morgan of Cotes") was appointed as [Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport") as an MP, but retained the office when she was appointed to the House of Lords.
* [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron"), Lord Cameron of Chipping Norton and a former Prime Minister, was appointed [Foreign Secretary](/wiki/Foreign_Secretary_(United_Kingdom) "Foreign Secretary (United Kingdom)") and a member of the House of Lords in 2023.[[89]](#cite_note-CameronLord-93)[[90]](#cite_note-94)

From 1999 to 2010 the [Attorney General for England and Wales](/wiki/Attorney_General_for_England_and_Wales "Attorney General for England and Wales") was a member of the House of Lords; as of July 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit), the Attorney General is once again a peer.

The House of Lords remains a source for junior ministers and members of government. Like the House of Commons, the Lords also has a [Government Chief Whip](/wiki/Captain_of_the_Honourable_Corps_of_Gentlemen-at-Arms "Captain of the Honourable Corps of Gentlemen-at-Arms") as well as several Junior Whips. Where a government department is not represented by a minister in the Lords or one is not available, government whips will act as spokesmen for them.[[91]](#cite_note-companion-95)

### Former judicial role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lords&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Former judicial role")]

Main article: [Judicial functions of the House of Lords](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords")

Historically, the House of Lords held several judicial functions. Most notably, until 2009 the House of Lords served as the [court of last resort](/wiki/Court_of_last_resort "Court of last resort") for most instances of UK law. Since 1 October 2009 this role is now held by the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom").

The Lords' judicial functions originated from the ancient role of the [Curia Regis](/wiki/Curia_Regis "Curia Regis") as a body that addressed the petitions of the King's subjects. The functions were exercised not by the whole House, but by a committee of "Law Lords". The bulk of the House's judicial business was conducted by the twelve [Lords of Appeal in Ordinary](/wiki/Lords_of_Appeal_in_Ordinary "Lords of Appeal in Ordinary"), who were specifically appointed for this purpose under the [Appellate Jurisdiction Act 1876](/wiki/Appellate_Jurisdiction_Act_1876 "Appellate Jurisdiction Act 1876").

The judicial functions could also be exercised by Lords of Appeal (other members of the House who happened to have held high judicial office). No Lord of Appeal in Ordinary or Lord of Appeal could sit judicially beyond the age of seventy-five. The judicial business of the Lords was supervised by the Senior Lord of Appeal in Ordinary and their deputy, the Second Senior Lord of Appeal in Ordinary.

The jurisdiction of the House of Lords extended, in civil and in criminal cases, to appeals from the courts of England and Wales, and of Northern Ireland. From Scotland, appeals were possible only in civil cases; Scotland's [High Court of Justiciary](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justiciary "High Court of Justiciary") is the highest court in criminal matters. The House of Lords was not the United Kingdom's only court of last resort; in some cases, the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") performs such a function. The jurisdiction of the Privy Council in the United Kingdom, however, is relatively restricted; it encompasses appeals from [ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical "Ecclesiastical") courts, disputes under the [House of Commons Disqualification Act 1975](/wiki/House_of_Commons_Disqualification_Act_1975 "House of Commons Disqualification Act 1975"), and a few other minor matters. Issues related to [devolution](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") were transferred from the Privy Council to the Supreme Court in 2009.

The twelve Law Lords did not all hear every case; rather, after World War II cases were heard by panels known as Appellate Committees, each of which normally consisted of five members (selected by the Senior Lord). An Appellate Committee hearing an important case could consist of more than five members. Though Appellate Committees met in separate committee rooms, judgement was given in the Lords Chamber itself. No further appeal lay from the House of Lords, although the House of Lords could refer a "preliminary question" to the [European Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") in cases involving an element of [European Union law](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law"), and a case could be brought at the [European Court of Human Rights](/wiki/European_Court_of_Human_Rights "European Court of Human Rights") if the House of Lords did not provide a satisfactory remedy in cases where the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") was relevant.

A distinct judicial function—one in which the whole House used to participate—is that of [trying impeachments](/wiki/Impeachment_trial "Impeachment trial"). [Impeachments](/wiki/Impeachment_in_the_United_Kingdom "Impeachment in the United Kingdom") were brought by the House of Commons, and tried in the House of Lords; a conviction required only a majority of the Lords voting. Impeachments, however, are to all intents and purposes obsolete; the last impeachment was that of [Henry Dundas, 1st Viscount Melville](/wiki/Henry_Dundas,_1st_Viscount_Melville "Henry Dundas, 1st Viscount Melville"), in 1806.

Similarly, the House of Lords was once the court that tried peers charged with high [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason") or [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony"). The House would be presided over not by the Lord Chancellor, but by the [Lord High Steward](/wiki/Lord_High_Steward "Lord High Steward"), an official especially appointed for the occasion of the trial. If Parliament was not in session, then peers could be tried in a separate court, known as the Lord High Steward's Court. Only peers, their wives, and their widows (unless remarried) were entitled to [such trials](/wiki/List_of_trials_of_peers_in_the_House_of_Lords "List of trials of peers in the House of Lords"); the Lords Spiritual were tried in ecclesiastical courts. In 1948, the right of peers to be tried in such special courts was abolished; now, they are tried in the regular courts.[[92]](#cite_note-96) The last such trial in the House was of [Edward Russell, 26th Baron de Clifford](/wiki/Edward_Russell,_26th_Baron_de_Clifford "Edward Russell, 26th Baron de Clifford"), in 1935. An illustrative dramatisation circa 1928 of a trial of a peer (the fictional [Duke of Denver](/wiki/Duke_of_Denver "Duke of Denver")) on a charge of murder (a felony) is portrayed in the 1972 BBC Television adaption of [Dorothy L. Sayers](/wiki/Dorothy_L._Sayers "Dorothy L. Sayers")' [Lord Peter Wimsey](/wiki/Lord_Peter_Wimsey "Lord Peter Wimsey") mystery *[Clouds of Witness](/wiki/Clouds_of_Witness "Clouds of Witness")*.

The [Constitutional Reform Act 2005](/wiki/Constitutional_Reform_Act_2005 "Constitutional Reform Act 2005") resulted in the creation of a separate Supreme Court of the United Kingdom, to which the judicial function of the House of Lords, and some of the judicial functions of the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council, were transferred. In addition, the office of Lord Chancellor was reformed by the act, removing his ability to act as both a government minister and a judge. This was motivated in part by concerns about the historical admixture of legislative, judicial, and executive power. The new Supreme Court is located at [Middlesex Guildhall](/wiki/Middlesex_Guildhall "Middlesex Guildhall").
